=== People Near Me ===
People Near Me (formerly People Nearby) is a peer-to-peer service designed to simplify communication and collaboration among users connected to the same subnet.
10147: People Near Me is used by Windows Meeting Space for collaboration and contact discovery.
10148: People Near Me uses Windows Contacts to manage contact information; by default, a user may receive invitations from all users connected to the same subnet, but a user can designate another user as a trusted contact to enable collaboration across the Internet, to increase security, and to determine the presence of these contacts.
10149: The new Background Intelligent Transfer Service (BITS) 3.0 in Windows Vista has a new feature called Neighbor Casting which supports peer-to-peer file transfers within a domain; this facilitates Peer Caching, allowing users to download and serve content (such as WSUS updates) from peers on the same subnet, receive notifications when a file is downloaded, access the temporary file while the download is in progress, and control HTTP redirects.
10150: BITS 3.0 also uses Internet Gateway Device Protocol counters to more accurately calculate available bandwidth.
10151: WinINet, the protocol handler for HTTP and FTP handles IPv6 literal addresses, includes support for Gzip and deflate decompression to improve content encoding performance, Internationalized domain names support and Event Tracing.
10152: WinHTTP, the client API for server-based applications and services supports IPv6, AutoProxy, HTTP/1.1 chunked transfer encoding, larger data uploads, SSL and client certificates, server and proxy authentication, automatic handling of redirects and keep-alive connections and HTTP/1.0 protocol, including support for keep-alive (persistent) connections and session cookies.
10153: While most of the same sockets programming concepts exist as in user-mode Winsock such as socket, creation, bind, connect, accept, send and receive, Winsock Kernel is a completely new programming interface with unique characteristics such as asynchronous I/O that uses IRPs and event callbacks to enhance performance.
10154: TDI is supported in Windows Vista for backward compatibility.
10155: Remote Differential Compression (RDC) is a client-server synchronization protocol allows data to be synchronized with a remote source using compression techniques to minimize the amount of data sent across the network.
10156: Therefore, RDC is suitable for efficient synchronization of files that have been updated independently, or when network bandwidth is small or in scenarios where the files are large but the differences between them are small.
10157: In Windows Vista, Bluetooth Devices is part of the Windows Shell itself (the previous Devices tab is no longer available as it is no longer necessary).
10158: New properties introduced for Bluetooth devices include Authenticated, Connection In Use, Discovery Method, Encrypted, Paired, and Version; users can group, sort, and search for devices based on these properties, and all properties are displayed in the new Details Pane.
10159: The Windows Vista Bluetooth stack supports a kernel mode device driver interface besides the user-mode programming interface, which enables third parties to add support for additional Bluetooth Profiles such as SCO, SDP, and L2CAP.
10160: The authentication mechanisms supported are PEAPv0/EAP-MSCHAPv2 (passwords) and PEAP-TLS (smartcards and certificates).
10161: Secure Socket Tunneling Protocol (SSTP), introduced in Windows Vista Service Pack 1 is a form of VPN tunnel that provides a mechanism to transport PPP or L2TP traffic through an SSL 3.0 channel.

On-board diagnostics (OBD) is a term referring to a vehicle's self-diagnostic and reporting capability. In the United States, this capability is a requirement to comply with federal emissions standards to detect failures that may increase the vehicle tailpipe emissions to more than 150% of the standard to which it was originally certified.
OBD systems give the vehicle owner or repair technician access to the status of the various vehicle sub-systems.

=== Startup Repair ===
Startup Repair (formerly System Recovery Troubleshooter Wizard) is a diagnostic feature designed to repair systems that cannot boot due to operating system corruption, incompatible drivers, or damaged hardware; it scans for corruption of operating system components such as Boot Configuration Data and the Windows Registry and also checks boot sectors, file system metadata, Master Boot Records, and partition tables for errors and whether the root cause for failure originated during an installation of Windows.

Packet loss occurs when one or more packets of data travelling across a computer network fail to reach their destination. Packet loss is either caused by errors in data transmission, typically across wireless networks, or network congestion. Packet loss is measured as a percentage of packets lost with respect to packets sent.
The Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) detects packet loss and performs retransmissions to ensure reliable messaging.
10371: Packet loss in a TCP connection is also used to avoid congestion and thus produces an intentionally reduced throughput for the connection.
10372: For example, with live streaming media, it is more important to deliver recent packets quickly than to ensure that stale packets are eventually delivered.
10373: An application or user may also decide to retry an operation that is taking a long time, in which case another set of packets will be added to the burden of delivering the original set.
10374: To avoid all of these problems, the Internet Protocol allows for routers to simply drop packets if the router or a network segment is too busy to deliver the data in a timely fashion.
10375: Dropping of packets acts as an implicit signal that the network is congested, and may cause senders to reduce the amount of bandwidth consumed, or attempt to find another path.
10376:
=== Wireless networks ===
Wireless networks are susceptible to a number of factors that can corrupt or lose packets in transit, such as radio frequency interference (RFI), radio signals that are too weak due to distance or multi-path fading, faulty networking hardware, or faulty network drivers.
10377: Cellular networks can experience packet loss caused by, "high bit error rate (BER), unstable channel characteristics, and user mobility." TCP's intentional throttling behavior prevents wireless networks from performing near their theoretical potential transfer rates because unmodified TCP treats all dropped packets as if they were caused by network congestion, and so may throttle wireless networks even when they aren't actually congested.
10378: If a single router or link is constraining the capacity of the complete travel path or of network travel in general, it is known as a bottleneck.
10379: In some cases, packets are intentionally dropped by routing routines, or through network dissuasion technique for operational management purposes.
10380: When reliable delivery is necessary, packet loss increases latency due to additional time needed for retransmission.
10381: The amount of packet loss that is acceptable depends on the type of data being sent.
10382:
== Packet recovery for reliable delivery ==
Per the end-to-end principle, the Internet Protocol leaves responsibility for packet recovery through the retransmission of dropped packets to the endpoints - the computers sending and receiving the data.
10383: In certain variants of TCP, if a transmitted packet is lost, it will be re-sent along with every packet that had already been sent after it.
10384: Applications that use UDP are expected to implement their own mechanisms for handling packet loss, if needed.
10385: Dropping packets is undesirable as the packet is either lost or must be retransmitted and this can impact real-time throughput; however, increasing the buffer size can lead to bufferbloat, which has its own impact on latency and jitter during congestion.
10386: In cases where quality of service is rate limiting a connection, e.g., using a leaky bucket algorithm, packets may be intentionally dropped in order to slow down specific services to ensure available bandwidth for other services marked with higher importance.
10387: For this reason, packet loss is not necessarily an indication of poor connection reliability or signs of a bandwidth bottleneck.

10389: Packet loss concealment (PLC) is a technique to mask the effects of packet loss in voice over IP (VoIP) communications. When the voice signal is sent as VoIP packets on an IP network, the packets may (and likely will) travel different routes. A packet therefore might arrive very late, might be corrupted, or simply might not arrive at all. One example case of the last situation could be, when a packet is rejected by a server which has a full buffer and cannot accept any more data.

10399: T.38 is an ITU recommendation for allowing transmission of fax over IP networks (FoIP) in real time.
10400: However, because most VoIP systems are optimized (through their use of aggressive lossy bandwidth-saving compression) for voice rather than data calls, conventional fax machines worked poorly or not at all on them due to the network impairments such as delay, jitter, packet loss, and so on.
10401: This particular type of device is called Internet-Aware Fax device, or IAF, and it is capable of initiating or completing a fax call towards the IP network.
10402: The typical scenario where T.38 is used is – T.38 fax relay – where a T.30 fax device sends a fax over PSTN to a T.38 fax gateway which converts or encapsulates the T.30 protocol into a T.38 data stream.
10403: The T.37 standard specifies how a fax image is encapsulated in e-mail and transported, ultimately, to the recipient using a store-and-forward process through intermediary entities.
10404: T.38, however, defines a protocol that supports the use of the T.30 protocol in both the sender and recipient terminals.
10405: A special protocol is needed for real-time fax over IP (Internet Protocol) since existing fax terminals only supported PSTN connections, where the information flow was generally smooth and uninterrupted, as opposed to the jittery arrival of  IP  packets.
10406: But facsimile data are transmitted by modems, which aren't as forgiving as the human ear is for speech.
10407: So the job of T.38 is to “fool” the terminal into “thinking” that it's communicating directly with another T.30 terminal.
10408: And since T.30 fax is a half-duplex protocol, the network is only needed for one direction at a time.

Professional video over IP systems use some existing standard video codec to reduce the program material to a bitstream (e.g., an MPEG transport stream), and then use an Internet Protocol (IP) network to carry that bitstream encapsulated in a stream of IP packets. This is typically accomplished using some variant of the RTP protocol.
Carrying professional video over IP networks has special challenges compared to most non-time-critical IP traffic.
10416: Many of these problems are similar to those encountered in voice over IP, but with more stringent engineering requirements.
10417: In particular, there are very strict quality of service requirements that must be fulfilled for use in professional broadcast environments.
10418: Resending packets is not an option because of the sequential nature of the underlying video signal.
10419: If forward error correction is used, a small proportion of packets arriving after the deadline can be tolerated as they can be discarded on receipt and treated in the same way as lost packets.
10420: An added time delay over 250 ms is particularly problematic with PTZ cameras as it makes operator control difficult.
10421: The extraction of coarse timing data is typically done using a phase locked loop with a long time constant.
10422: One approach to this is the quality of service approach, which simply allocates sufficient bandwidth to video-carrying traffic that it will not congest under any possible load pattern.
10423: Other approaches include dynamic reduction in frame rate or resolution, Network Admission Control, bandwidth reservation, traffic shaping, and traffic prioritization techniques, which require more complex network engineering, but will work when the simple approach of building a non-blocking network is not possible.
10424: One key advantage of IP-based CCTV is the ability to use network infrastructure, providing adequate bandwidth and availability of switching and routing, rather than coaxial cabling.
10425: Digital video also makes possible Video Content Analysis, which allows automatic detection and identification of various kinds of objects or motion.
10426: Another emerging model is off-site storage of surveillance video.
10427: Manufacturers of CCTV equipment have been integrating IP network technology into their product ranges.

Ping is a computer network administration software utility used to test the reachability of a host on an Internet Protocol (IP) network. It is available in a wide range of operating systems, including most embedded network administration software.
Ping measures the round-trip time for messages sent from the originating host to a destination computer that are echoed back to the source.

In computing, Mosh (mobile shell) is a tool used to connect from a client computer to a server over the Internet, to run a remote terminal. Mosh is similar to SSH, with additional features meant to improve usability for mobile users. The major features are:

10473: Quality of service (QoS) is the description or measurement of the overall performance of a service, such as a telephony or computer network, or a cloud computing service, particularly the performance seen by the users of the network. 10526: In computer science, program optimization, code optimization, or software optimization is the process of modifying a software system to make some aspect of it work more efficiently or use fewer resources. In general, a computer program may be optimized so that it executes more rapidly, or to make it capable of operating with less memory storage or other resources, or draw less power.
10527: One frequently cited example is the space–time tradeoff,  where reducing a program’s execution time can increase its memory consumption.
10528: Conversely, in scenarios where memory is limited, engineers might prioritize a slower algorithm to conserve space.
10529: Typically the higher levels have greater impact, and are harder to change later on in a project, requiring significant changes or a complete rewrite if they need to be changed.
10530: Typically some consideration is given to efficiency throughout a project –  though this varies significantly –  but major optimization is often considered a refinement to be done late, if ever.
10531: On longer-running projects there are typically cycles of optimization, where improving one area reveals limitations in another, and these are typically curtailed when performance is acceptable or gains become too small or costly.
10532: As performance is part of the specification of a program –  a program that is unusably slow is not fit for purpose: a video game with 60 Hz (frames-per-second) might be acceptable, but 6 frames-per-second is unacceptably choppy –  performance is a consideration from the start, to ensure that the system is able to deliver sufficient performance, and early prototypes need to have roughly acceptable performance for there to be confidence that the final system will (with optimization) achieve acceptable performance.
10533: For example, a system that is network latency-bound (where network latency is the main constraint on overall performance) would be optimized to minimize network trips, ideally making a single request (or no requests, as in a push protocol) rather than multiple roundtrips.
10534: Generally data structures are more difficult to change than algorithms, as a data structure assumption and its performance assumptions are used throughout the program, though this can be minimized by the use of abstract data types in function definitions, and keeping the concrete data structure definitions restricted to a few places.
10535: For algorithms, this primarily consists of ensuring that algorithms are constant O(1), logarithmic O(log n), linear O(n), or in some cases log-linear O(n log n) in the input (both in space and time).
10536: A good example is the use of a fast path for common cases, improving performance by avoiding unnecessary work.
10537: Another important technique is caching, particularly memoization, which avoids redundant computations.
10538: Because of the importance of caching, there are often many levels of caching in a system, which can cause problems from memory use, and correctness issues from stale caches.
10539: Loop-invariant code motion and return value optimization are examples of optimizations that reduce the need for auxiliary variables and can even result in faster performance by avoiding round-about optimizations.
10540: Programs (other than very small programs) are seldom written from start to finish in assembly due to the time and cost involved.
10541: Most are compiled down from a high level language to assembly and hand optimized from there.
10542: When efficiency and size are less important large parts may be written in a high-level language.
10543: With more modern optimizing compilers and the greater complexity of recent CPUs, it is harder to write more efficient code than what the compiler generates, and few projects need this "ultimate" optimization step.
10544: Much of the code written today is intended to run on as many machines as possible.

10547: While the latter ones are effective on most or all platforms, platform-dependent techniques use specific properties of one platform, or rely on parameters depending on the single platform or even on the single processor.
10548: A more efficient version with equivalent functionality is known as a strength reduction.

10552: This will usually require a trade-off –  where one factor is optimized at the expense of others.
10553: Such changes are sometimes jokingly referred to as pessimizations.

10589: A Network Coordinate System (NC system) is a system for predicting characteristics such as the latency or bandwidth of connections between nodes in a network by assigning coordinates to nodes.
10590:
== Uses ==
In general, Network Coordinate Systems can be used for peer discovery, optimal-server selection, and characteristic-aware routing.

10612: Memory timings or RAM timings describe the timing information of a memory module or the onboard LPDDRx. Due to the inherent qualities of VLSI and microelectronics, memory chips require time to fully execute commands. Executing commands too quickly will result in data corruption and results in system instability.
10613: Due to the inherent qualities of VLSI and microelectronics, memory chips require time to fully execute commands.
10614: The timing of modern synchronous dynamic random-access memory (SDRAM) is commonly indicated using four parameters: CL, TRCD, TRP, and TRAS in units of clock cycles; they are commonly written as four numbers separated with hyphens, e.g.
10615: Or a fifth, the Command rate, is sometimes added (normally 2T or 1T, also written 2N, 1N or CR2).
10616: The Intel systems also have Gear 2 (Gear type 0) and Gear 4 (Gear type 1).
10617: What determines absolute latency (and thus system performance) is determined by both the timings and the memory clock frequency.
10618: When translating memory timings into actual latency, timings are in units of clock cycles, which for double data rate memory is half the speed of the commonly quoted transfer rate.
10619: Without knowing the clock frequency it is impossible to state if one set of timings is "faster" than another.
10620: Faster DDR3-2666 memory (with a 1333 MHz clock, or 0.75 ns exactly; the 1333 is rounded) may have a larger CAS latency of 9, but at a clock frequency of 1333 MHz the amount of time to wait 9 clock cycles is only 6.75 ns.
10621: The full, manufacturer-verified timings of a memory module are stored inside of a module's Serial Presence Detect (SPD) chip.
10622: On DDR3 and DDR4 DIMM modules, this chip is a PROM or EEPROM flash memory chip and contains the JEDEC-standardized timing table data format.
10623: See the SPD article for the table layout among different versions of DDR and examples of other memory timing information that is present on these chips.
10624: On some DIMM modules, there are also "verified" overclocking profiles in the SPD using XMP or Expo formats.

10626: DDR4 introduced support for FGR (fine granular refresh), with its own tRFC2 and tRFC4 timings, while DDR5 retained only tRFC2.
10627: By interleaving access to SDRAM's multiple internal banks, it is possible to transfer data continuously at the peak transfer rate.
10628: In particular, each successive generation of DDR memory has higher transfer rates but the absolute latency does not change significantly, and especially when first appearing on the market, the new generation generally has longer latency than the previous one.
10629: Modern x86 processors are heavily optimized with techniques such as superscalar instruction pipelines, out-of-order execution, memory prefetching, memory dependence prediction, and branch prediction to preemptively load memory from RAM (and other caches) to speed up execution even further.
10630: With this amount of complexity from performance optimization, it is difficult to state with certainty the effects memory timings may have on performance.

10635: In computer science and particularly in compiler design, loop nest optimization (LNO) is an optimization technique that applies a set of loop transformations for the purpose of locality optimization or parallelization or another loop overhead reduction of the loop nests.  (Nested loops occur when one loop is inside of another loop.) One classical usage is to reduce memory access latency or the cache bandwidth necessary due to cache reuse for some common linear algebra algorithms.

10675: Multi-objective optimization or Pareto optimization (also known as multi-objective programming, vector optimization, multicriteria optimization, or multiattribute optimization) is an area of multiple-criteria decision making that is concerned with mathematical optimization problems involving more than one objective function to be optimized simultaneously.
10676: Multi-objective is a type of vector optimization that has been applied in many fields of science, including engineering, economics and logistics where optimal decisions need to be taken in the presence of trade-offs between two or more conflicting objectives.
10677: The objective functions are said to be conflicting.
10678: A solution is called nondominated, Pareto optimal, Pareto efficient or noninferior, if none of the objective functions can be improved in value without degrading some of the other objective values.
10679: The goal may be to find a representative set of Pareto optimal solutions, and/or quantify the trade-offs in satisfying the different objectives, and/or finding a single solution that satisfies the subjective preferences of a human decision maker (DM).
10680: Bicriteria optimization denotes the special case in which there are two objective functions.
10681: The feasible set is typically defined by some constraint functions.
10682: If some objective function is to be maximized, it is equivalent to minimize its negative or its inverse.
10683: Therefore, attention is paid to Pareto optimal solutions; that is, solutions that cannot be improved in any of the objectives without degrading at least one of the other objectives.
10684: The Pareto front of a multi-objective optimization problem is bounded by a so-called nadir objective vector.
10685: is a small constant, is often defined because of numerical reasons.
10686: For example, consumer's demand for various goods is determined by the process of maximization of the utilities derived from those goods, subject to a constraint based on how much income is available to spend on those goods and on the prices of those goods.
10687: This constraint allows more of one good to be purchased only at the sacrifice of consuming less of another good; therefore, the various objectives (more consumption of each good is preferred) are in conflict with each other.
10688: A common method for analyzing such a problem is to use a graph of indifference curves, representing preferences, and a budget constraint, representing the trade-offs that the consumer is faced with.
10689: The frontier specifies the trade-offs that the society is faced with — if the society is fully utilizing its resources, more of one good can be produced only at the expense of producing less of another good.
10690: Macroeconomic policy-making is a context requiring multi-objective optimization.
10691: To do this, the central bank uses a model of the economy that quantitatively describes the various causal linkages in the economy; it simulates the model repeatedly under various possible stances of monetary policy, in order to obtain a menu of possible predicted outcomes for the various variables of interest.
10692: This problem is often represented by a graph in which the efficient frontier shows the best combinations of risk and expected return that are available, and in which indifference curves show the investor's preferences for various risk-expected return combinations.
10693: In engineering and economics, many problems involve multiple objectives which are not describable as the-more-the-better or the-less-the-better; instead, there is an ideal target value for each objective, and the desire is to get as close as possible to the desired value of each objective.
10694: Often such problems are subject to linear equality constraints that prevent all objectives from being simultaneously perfectly met, especially when the number of controllable variables is less than the number of objectives and when the presence of random shocks generates uncertainty.

10767: Cilium is a cloud native technology for networking, observability, and security. It is based on the kernel technology eBPF, originally for better networking performance, and now leverages many additional features for different use cases.
10768: Cilium is a cloud native technology for networking, observability, and security.
10769: The core networking component has evolved from only providing a flat Layer 3 network for containers to including advanced networking features, like BGP and Service mesh, within a Kubernetes cluster, across multiple clusters, and connecting with the world outside Kubernetes.
10770: Cilium runs on Linux and is one of the first eBPF applications being ported to Microsoft Windows through the eBPF on Windows project.

May 2016 - Network policy was added, expanding the scope beyond just networking

10775: Cilium’s eBPF based dataplane provides a simple flat Layer 3 network with the ability to span multiple clusters in either a native routing or overlay mode with Cilium Cluster Mesh.

In computer architecture, Amdahl's law (or Amdahl's argument) is a formula limiting the speedup of a task as resources are added to the system executing that task.
The law can be stated as:

the overall performance improvement gained by optimizing a single part of a system is limited by the fraction of time that the improved part is actually used.
10780: In computer architecture, Amdahl's law (or Amdahl's argument) is a formula limiting the speedup of a task as resources are added to the system executing that task.
10781: the overall performance improvement gained by optimizing a single part of a system is limited by the fraction of time that the improved part is actually used.
10782: It is named after computer scientist Gene Amdahl, and was presented at the American Federation of Information Processing Societies (AFIPS) Spring Joint Computer Conference in 1967.
10783: will reach 100 if 100% of the execution time is covered) is lost due to the remaining 1% of execution time that was not improved.
10785: For example, assume that we are given a serial task which is split into four consecutive parts, whose percentages of execution time are p1 = 0.11, p2 = 0.18, p3 = 0.23, and p4 = 0.48 respectively.
10786: Then we are told that the 1st part is not sped up, so s1 = 1, while the 2nd part is sped up 5 times, so s2 = 5, the 3rd part is sped up 20 times, so s3 = 20, and the 4th part is sped up 1.6 times, so s4 = 1.6.
10787: For example, with a serial program in two parts A and B for which TA = 3 s and TB = 1 s,.
10788: if part B is made to run 5 times faster, that is s = 5 and p = TB/(TA + TB) = 0.25, then.
10789: if part A is made to run 2 times faster, that is s = 2 and p = TA/(TA + TB) = 0.75, then.
10790: Therefore, making part A to run 2 times faster is better than making part B to run 5 times faster.

10793: If one picks optimally (in terms of the achieved speedup) what is to be improved, then one will see monotonically decreasing improvements as one improves.
10794: Note that it is often rational to improve a system in an order that is "non-optimal" in this sense, given that some improvements are more difficult or require larger development time than others.
10795: Amdahl's law does represent the law of diminishing returns if one is considering what sort of return one gets by adding more processors to a machine, if one is running a fixed-size computation that will use all available processors to their capacity.
10796: An implication of Amdahl's law is that to speed up real applications which have both serial and parallel portions, heterogeneous computing techniques are required.

TDM refers to a T1, E1, T3 or E3 signal, while the PSN is based either on IP or MPLS or on raw Ethernet. A related technology is circuit emulation, which enables transport of TDM traffic over cell-based (ATM) networks.

10852: Monitor mode, or RFMON (Radio Frequency MONitor) mode, allows a computer with a wireless network interface controller (WNIC) to monitor all traffic received on a wireless channel. Unlike promiscuous mode, which is also used for packet sniffing, monitor mode allows packets to be captured without having to associate with an access point or ad hoc network first. Monitor mode only applies to wireless networks, while promiscuous mode can be used on both wired and wireless networks.

== Uses ==
Uses for monitor mode include: geographical packet analysis, observing of widespread traffic and acquiring knowledge of Wi-Fi technology through hands-on experience.

The Microsoft Windows Network Driver Interface Specification (NDIS) API has supported extensions for monitor mode since NDIS version 6, first available in Windows Vista.
10857: NDIS 6 supports exposing 802.11 frames to the upper protocol levels, while previous versions only exposed fake Ethernet frames translated from the 802.11 frames.
10858: Monitor mode support in NDIS 6 is an optional feature and may or may not be implemented in the client adapter driver.
10859: In many cases, monitor mode support is not properly implemented by the vendor.
10860: STA drivers (Ralink, Broadcom) and every other manufacturer’s provided driver doesn’t support monitor mode.

10863: In telecommunications and computer networking, a network packet is a formatted unit of data carried by a packet-switched network. A packet consists of control information and user data; the latter is also known as the payload. Control information provides data for delivering the payload (e.g., source and destination network addresses, error detection codes, or sequencing information). Typically, control information is found in packet headers and trailers.
10864: In telecommunications and computer networking, a network packet is a formatted unit of data carried by a packet-switched network.
10865: A packet consists of control information and user data; the latter is also known as the payload.
10866: In packet switching, the bandwidth of the transmission medium is shared between multiple communication sessions, in contrast to circuit switching, in which circuits are preallocated for the duration of one session and data is typically transmitted as a continuous bit stream.
10867: In layer 4, the transport layer, the data units are segments and datagrams.
10868: Thus, in the example of TCP/IP communication over Ethernet, a TCP segment is carried in one or more IP packets, which are each carried in one or more Ethernet frames.
10869:
Addresses
The routing of network packets requires two network addresses, the source address of the sending host, and the destination address of the receiving host.
10870: At the transmitter, the calculation is performed before the packet is sent.
10871: When received at the destination, the checksum is recalculated, and compared with the one in the packet.
10872: If discrepancies are found, the packet may be corrected or discarded.
10873: Any packet loss due to these discards is dealt with by the network protocol.
10874: Time to live is a field that is decreased by one each time a packet goes through a network hop.
10875: Ethernet packets have no time-to-live field and so are subject to broadcast storms in the presence of a switching loop.

Length

10877: However, in some types of networks, the length is implied by the duration of the transmission.
10878: A protocol identifier field specifies a packet's protocol and allows the protocol stack to process many types of packets.

Priority
Some networks implement quality of service which can prioritize some types of packets above others.
10880: This field indicates which packet queue should be used; a high-priority queue is emptied more quickly than lower-priority queues at points in the network where congestion is occurring.
10881:
Payload
In general, the payload is the data that is carried on behalf of an application.

=== MPEG packetized stream ===
Packetized elementary stream (PES) is a specification associated with the MPEG-2 standard that allows an elementary stream to be divided into packets.
10886: The elementary stream is packetized by encapsulating sequential data bytes from the elementary stream between PES packet headers.
10887: A typical method of transmitting elementary stream data from a video or audio encoder is to first create PES packets from the elementary stream data and then to encapsulate these PES packets inside an MPEG transport stream (TS) packets or an MPEG program stream (PS).
10888:
=== NICAM ===
In order to provide mono compatibility, the NICAM signal is transmitted on a subcarrier alongside the sound carrier.
10889: This means that the FM or AM regular mono sound carrier is left alone for reception by monaural receivers.
10890: The NICAM packet (except for the header) is scrambled with a nine-bit pseudo-random bit-generator before transmission.
10891: Making the NICAM bitstream look more like white noise is important because this reduces signal patterning on adjacent TV channels.

Logic optimization is a process of finding an equivalent representation of the specified logic circuit under one or more specified constraints. This process is a part of a logic synthesis applied in digital electronics and integrated circuit design.
Generally, the circuit is constrained to a minimum chip area meeting a predefined response delay. The goal of logic optimization of a given circuit is to obtain the smallest logic circuit that evaluates to the same values as the original one.
11162: This process is a part of a logic synthesis applied in digital electronics and integrated circuit design.
11163: Generally, the circuit is constrained to a minimum chip area meeting a predefined response delay.
11164: The goal of logic optimization of a given circuit is to obtain the smallest logic circuit that evaluates to the same values as the original one.
11165: Usually, the smaller circuit with the same function is cheaper, takes less space, consumes less power, has shorter latency, and minimizes risks of unexpected cross-talk, hazard of delayed signal processing, and other issues present at the nano-scale level of metallic structures on an integrated circuit.
11166: In terms of Boolean algebra, the optimization of a complex Boolean expression is a process of finding a simpler one, which would upon evaluation ultimately produce the same results as the original one.

11168: With the advent of logic synthesis, one of the biggest challenges faced by the electronic design automation (EDA) industry was to find the most simple circuit representation of the given design description.
11169: While two-level logic optimization had long existed in the form of the Quine–McCluskey algorithm, later followed by the Espresso heuristic logic minimizer, the rapidly improving chip densities, and the wide adoption of Hardware description languages for circuit description, formalized the logic optimization domain as it exists today, including Logic Friday (graphical interface), Minilog, and ESPRESSO-IISOJS (many-valued logic).

=== Two-level versus multi-level representations ===
While a two-level circuit representation of circuits strictly refers to the flattened view of the circuit in terms of SOPs (sum-of-products) — which is more applicable to a PLA implementation of the design — a multi-level representation is a more generic view of the circuit in terms of arbitrarily connected SOPs, POSs (product-of-sums), factored form etc.
11176: In sum-of-products (SOP) form, AND gates form the smallest unit and are stitched together using ORs, whereas in product-of-sums (POS) form it is opposite.
11177: POS form requires parentheses to group the OR terms together under AND gates, because OR has lower precedence than AND.
11178: The above 2-level representation takes six product terms and 24 transistors in CMOS Rep.
11179: While the number of levels here is 3, the total number of product terms and literals reduce  because of the sharing of the term B + C.
11180: Some examples are priority encoders, binary decoders, multiplexers, demultiplexers.

11182: It is evident that two negations, two conjunctions, and a disjunction are used in this statement.

My traceroute, originally named Matt's traceroute (MTR), is a computer program that combines the functions of the traceroute and ping programs in one network diagnostic tool.
MTR probes routers on the route path by limiting the number of hops individual packets may traverse, and listening to responses of their expiry. It will regularly repeat this process, usually once per second, and keep track of the response times of the hops along the path.

11375: Satellite Internet access is Internet access provided through communication satellites; if it can sustain high speeds, it is termed satellite broadband. Modern consumer grade satellite Internet service is typically provided to individual users through geostationary satellites that can offer relatively high data speeds, with newer satellites using the Ku band to achieve downstream data speeds up to 506 Mbit/s.
11376: Modern consumer grade satellite Internet service is typically provided to individual users through geostationary satellites that can offer relatively high data speeds, with newer satellites using the Ku band to achieve downstream data speeds up to 506 Mbit/s.
11377: In addition, new satellite internet constellations are being developed in low-earth orbit to enable low-latency internet access from space.
11378: The first commercial communications satellite was Telstar 1, built by Bell Labs and launched in July 1962.
11379: The idea of a geosynchronous satellite—one that could orbit the Earth above the equator and remain fixed by following the Earth's rotation—was first proposed by Herman Potočnik in 1928 and popularised by the science fiction author Arthur C.
11380: Following the invention of the Internet and the World Wide Web, geostationary satellites attracted interest as a potential means of providing Internet access.

OpenVPN is a virtual private network (VPN) system that implements techniques to create secure point-to-point or site-to-site connections in routed or bridged configurations and remote access facilities. It implements both client and server applications.
OpenVPN allows peers to authenticate each other using pre-shared secret keys, certificates or username/password.

11790: Internet Protocol version 6 (IPv6) is the most recent version of the Internet Protocol (IP), the communications protocol that provides an identification and location system for computers on networks and routes traffic across the Internet. IPv6 was developed by the Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) to deal with the long-anticipated problem of IPv4 address exhaustion, and was intended to replace IPv4.
11791: Internet Protocol version 6 (IPv6) is the most recent version of the Internet Protocol (IP), the communications protocol that provides an identification and location system for computers on networks and routes traffic across the Internet.
11792: Devices on the Internet are assigned a unique IP address for identification and location definition.
11793: IPv6 provides other technical benefits in addition to a larger addressing space.
11794: The use of multicast addressing is expanded and simplified, and provides additional optimization for the delivery of services.
11795: IPv6 addresses are represented as eight groups of four hexadecimal digits each, separated by colons.
11796: The IPv6 subnet size is standardized by fixing the size of the host identifier portion of an address to 64 bits.
11797: The addressing architecture of IPv6 allows three different types of transmission: unicast, anycast and multicast.
11798: IPv4 includes an addressing system that uses numerical identifiers consisting of 32 bits.
11799: These addresses are typically displayed in dot-decimal notation as decimal values of four octets, each in the range 0 to 255, or 8 bits per number.
11800: Thus, IPv4 provides an addressing capability of 232 or approximately 4.3 billion addresses.
11801: At that point, each RIR still had available address pools and was expected to continue with standard address allocation policies until one /8 Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR) block remained.
11802: Because the headers of IPv4 packets and IPv6 packets are significantly different, the two protocols are not interoperable.
11803: However, most transport and application-layer protocols need little or no change to operate over IPv6; exceptions are application protocols that embed Internet-layer addresses, such as File Transfer Protocol (FTP) and Network Time Protocol (NTP), where the new address format may cause conflicts with existing protocol syntax.
11804: The size of an IPv6 address is 128 bits, compared to 32 bits in IPv4.
11805: Some blocks of this space and some specific addresses are reserved for special uses.
11806: While this address space is very large, it was not the intent of the designers of IPv6 to assure geographical saturation with usable addresses.
11807: The network identifier part of the address in IPv6 is usually 264 addresses, about four billion times the size of the entire IPv4 address space.
11808: In IPv6, the same result is achieved by sending a packet to the link-local all nodes multicast group at address ff02::1, which is analogous to IPv4 multicasting to address 224.0.0.1.
11809: IPv6 also provides for new multicast implementations, including embedding rendezvous point addresses in an IPv6 multicast group address, which simplifies the deployment of inter-domain solutions.
11810: In IPv4 it is very difficult for an organization to get even one globally routable multicast group assignment, and the implementation of inter-domain solutions is arcane.
11811: With such an assignment it is possible to embed the unicast address prefix into the IPv6 multicast address format, while still providing a 32-bit block, the least significant bits of the address, or approximately 4.2 billion multicast group identifiers.
11812: When necessary, hosts may configure additional stateful addresses via Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol version 6 (DHCPv6) or static addresses manually.
11813: Like IPv4, IPv6 supports globally unique IP addresses.
11814: Therefore, every device on the network is globally addressable directly from any other device.
11815: Therefore, such addresses are a particular privacy concern for mobile devices, such as laptops and cell phones.

The technique was initially used to bypass the need to assign a new address to every host when a network was moved, or when the upstream Internet service provider was replaced but could not route the network's address space.

WireGuard is a communication protocol and free and open-source software that implements encrypted virtual private networks (VPNs). It aims to be lighter and better performing than IPsec and OpenVPN, two common tunneling protocols. The WireGuard protocol passes traffic over UDP.
In March 2020, the Linux version of the software reached a stable production release and was incorporated into the Linux 5.6 kernel, and backported to earlier Linux kernels in some Linux distributions.
11952: The Linux kernel components are licensed under the GNU General Public License (GPL) version 2; other implementations are under GPLv2 or other free/open-source licenses.
11953:
== Protocol ==
The WireGuard protocol is a variant of the Noise Protocol Framework IK handshake pattern, as illustrated by the choice of Noise_IKpsk2_25519_ChaChaPoly_BLAKE2s for the value of the Construction string listed on p10 of the Whitepaper.
11954: Once the keys have been confirmed by both peers, the Noise Protocol is used to generate a shared ChaCha20 session key for symmetric encryption authenticated with Poly1305.
11955: SipHash24 is used for hashtable keys while BLAKE2s cryptographic hash functions, a faster and more compact version of SHA-3, are incorporated.
11956: Key derivation functions are handled using HKDF and Base64-encoded private keys, public keys and preshared keys.
11957: WireGuard’s proof starts by modeling its two-message Noise-based handshake (plus optional PSK) in CryptoVerif’s calculus of cryptographic games, abstracting ChaCha20-Poly1305, Curve25519, HKDF, the hash chain, and related primitives under standard IND-CPA/INT-CTXT and random-oracle assumptions.
11958: From that model, CryptoVerif’s automated game hops show, across unlimited parallel sessions, that the protocol guarantees mutual authentication, IND-CCA session-key secrecy, forward secrecy and post-compromise security even if long-term keys later leak and state is wiped.
11959: In May 2019, researchers from INRIA published a machine-checked proof of the WireGuard protocol, produced using the CryptoVerif proof assistant.
11960: This addresses the risk that traffic may be stored until quantum computers are capable of breaking Curve25519, at which point traffic could be decrypted.
11961: Pre-shared keys are "usually troublesome from a key management perspective and might be more likely stolen", but in the shorter term, if the symmetric key is compromised, the Curve25519 keys still provide more than sufficient protection.
11962: Tunneling TCP over a TCP-based connection is known as "TCP-over-TCP", and doing so can induce a dramatic loss in transmission performance due to the TCP meltdown problem.
11963: WireGuard fully supports IPv6, both inside and outside of tunnel.
11964: Hosts often avoid sending packets > 1280 due to PMTUD reliability.
11965: The MTU of a WireGuard interface is determined by the encapsulating, or outer, IP protocol—not the IP version carried inside the tunnel.
11966: When WireGuard packets are transported over IPv4, the outer-header overhead is 60 bytes (20-byte IPv4 header, 8-byte UDP header, and 32-byte WireGuard header).
11967: This distinction means that even if a peer is reachable only via IPv4, other peers in the same mesh may connect over IPv6 or through translation mechanisms.
11968: If a flaw is found in any of the primitives, a new version can be released that resolves the issue.

Psiphon is a free and open-source Internet censorship circumvention tool that uses a combination of secure communication and obfuscation technologies, such as a VPN, SSH, and a Web proxy. Psiphon is a centrally managed and geographically diverse network of thousands of proxy servers, using a performance-oriented, single- and multi-hop routing architecture.
Psiphon is specifically designed to support users in countries considered to be "enemies of the Internet".
12007: The codebase is developed and maintained by Psiphon, Inc., which operates systems and technologies designed to assist Internet users to securely bypass the content-filtering systems used by governments to impose censorship of the Internet.
12008: The original concept for Psiphon (1.0) was developed by the Citizen Lab at the University of Toronto, building upon previous generations of web proxy software systems, such as the "Safe Web" and "Anonymizer" systems.
12009: Psiphon 1.X is no longer supported by Psiphon, Inc.
12010: According to Nart Villeneuve, "The idea is to get (users) to install this on their computer, and then deliver the location of that circumventor, to people in filtered countries by the means they know to be the most secure.
12011: What we're trying to build is a network of trust among people who know each other, rather than a large tech network that people can just tap into." Psiphon 1.0 was launched by the Citizen Lab on 1 December 2006 as open-source software.
12012: In 2008, Psiphon was awarded the Netexplorateur award by the French Senate.
12013: In 2009, Psiphon was recognized with The Economist Best New Media Award by Index on Censorship.
12014: In 2011, Psiphon 1.X was officially retired and is no longer actively supported by Psiphon, Inc., or the Citizen Lab.

12051: SafeWeb, Inc. was an American internet privacy and computer security company based in Emeryville, California, that operated from 2000 to 2003. SafeWeb ran a free web anonymization service and built TriangleBoy, a censorship circumvention tool funded by the CIA's In-Q-Tel. After the free service shut down in late 2001, the company pivoted to SSL VPN appliances. Symantec acquired SafeWeb for $26 million in cash in October 2003.
12052:
=== Founding ===
SafeWeb was co-founded by Stephen Hsu, Jon Chun, and James Hormuzdiar.
12053: The service became popular with users in countries with internet censorship, including China, Iran, and Saudi Arabia.

12055:
=== Triangle Boy and censorship circumvention ===
SafeWeb developed TriangleBoy, a software tool designed to circumvent internet censorship by governments.
12056: In-Q-Tel funded the development of Triangle Boy, and the Broadcasting Board of Governors (BBG) provided funding to SafeWeb to set up proxy servers specifically to help Chinese internet users access Voice of America and Radio Free Asia websites, which were regularly blocked by the Chinese government.
12057:
Hsu had previously presented Triangle Boy at DEF CON 9 in July 2001 in Las Vegas, in a talk titled "SafeWeb's Triangle Boy: IP Spoofing and Strong Encryption in Service of a Free Internet."
The Chinese government responded by blocking SafeWeb's servers, in what Voice of America described as a "cat-and-mouse game." Clayton, Murdoch, and Watson (2006) described Triangle Boy as an early anti-censorship technique that used distributed proxies to evade IP-based blocking.
12058: SafeWeb pivoted to the enterprise security market with the Secure Extranet Appliance (SEA) Tsunami, a rack-mounted hardware appliance that provided secure remote access over the internet using SSL/TLS encryption built into standard web browsers.

==== BEAST attack ====
On September 23, 2011, researchers Thai Duong and Juliano Rizzo demonstrated a proof of concept called BEAST (Browser Exploit Against SSL/TLS) using a Java applet to violate same origin policy constraints, for a long-known cipher block chaining (CBC) vulnerability in TLS 1.0: an attacker observing 2 consecutive ciphertext blocks C0, C1 can test if the plaintext block P1 is equal to x by choosing the next plaintext block P2 = x ⊕ C0 ⊕ C1; as per CBC operation, C2 = E(C1 ⊕ P2) = E(C1 ⊕ x ⊕ C0 ⊕ C1) = E(C0 ⊕ x), which will be equal to C1 if x = P1.

12133: Forward secrecy is a property of cryptographic systems which ensures that a session key derived from a set of public and private keys will not be compromised if one of the private keys is compromised in the future.
12134: Without forward secrecy, if the server's private key is compromised, not only will all future TLS-encrypted sessions using that server certificate be compromised, but also any past sessions that used it as well (provided that these past sessions were intercepted and stored at the time of transmission).
12135: In practice, unless a web service uses Diffie–Hellman key exchange to implement forward secrecy, all of the encrypted web traffic to and from that service can be decrypted by a third party if it obtains the server's master (private) key; e.g., by means of a court order.

12138: TLS/HTTPS interception is used as an information security measure by network operators in order to be able to scan for and protect against the intrusion of malicious content into the network, such as computer viruses and other malware.
12139: Such content could otherwise not be detected as long as it is protected by encryption, which is increasingly the case as a result of the routine use of HTTPS and other secure protocols.
12140: A significant drawback of TLS/HTTPS interception is that it introduces new security risks of its own.
12141: One notable limitation is that it provides a point where network traffic is available unencrypted thus giving attackers an incentive to attack this point in particular in order to gain access to otherwise secure content.
12142: The interception also allows the network operator, or persons who gain access to its interception system, to perform man-in-the-middle attacks against network users.

12209: The TCP three-way handshake establishes a connection using three steps: (1) SYN — the client sends a segment with the SYN flag and an initial sequence number (ISN); (2) SYN-ACK — the server responds with SYN+ACK, acknowledging the client's ISN and advertising its own ISN; (3) ACK — the client acknowledges the server's ISN, completing the connection setup. After this exchange both sides have agreed on sequence numbers and the connection is established. A four-way FIN handshake tears it down gracefully.
12210: TCP congestion control prevents a sender from overwhelming the network by dynamically adjusting the sending rate. It uses a congestion window (cwnd) that starts small (slow start) and grows exponentially until a threshold (ssthresh) is reached, then linearly (congestion avoidance). When packet loss is detected (via timeout or duplicate ACKs), the window shrinks. Modern variants include CUBIC (Linux default), BBR (Google), and RENO. Understanding congestion control helps explain throughput variability and why latency spikes precede packet loss.
12211: TCP Reno uses Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease (AIMD): it grows cwnd by one MSS per RTT and halves it on loss. TCP CUBIC, the Linux default since kernel 2.6.19, uses a cubic function to set cwnd based on time elapsed since the last loss event, recovering faster on high-bandwidth, high-latency links. CUBIC is more aggressive in claiming available bandwidth and is preferred for modern high-speed networks, while Reno is simpler and fairer on low-latency links.
12212: BGP route hijacking occurs when an Autonomous System (AS) originates or propagates more-specific prefixes that it does not legitimately own, redirecting internet traffic through the attacker's infrastructure. Detection relies on route monitoring services (RIPE RIS, BGPmon, Cloudflare Radar) that compare observed BGP announcements against a registry of expected origin ASes. Mitigation uses RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure) with Route Origin Authorizations (ROAs), which cryptographically bind an IP prefix to its authorized origin AS, allowing routers to drop invalid announcements.
12213: RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure) is a cryptographic framework that lets IP address holders create signed Route Origin Authorizations (ROAs) attesting which Autonomous Systems are authorized to originate their prefixes. Routers with RPKI-enabled software (e.g., Routinator, FORT Validator) download these ROAs and can mark BGP routes as Valid, Invalid, or NotFound, then drop Invalid routes. RPKI eliminates a large class of BGP hijack and route-leak incidents without requiring any changes to the BGP protocol itself.
12214: OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) is a link-state protocol where every router floods Link State Advertisements (LSAs) to build an identical topology database across the area. Each router then runs Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm on its local copy to calculate the lowest-cost path tree rooted at itself. Cost is typically inversely proportional to bandwidth (reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth). SPF recalculates only when the topology changes, and OSPF areas limit the blast radius of LSA flooding in large networks.
12215: CGNAT (Carrier-Grade NAT) is NAT performed by an ISP on its own infrastructure, placing multiple customers behind a single public IPv4 address using the RFC 6598 range (100.64.0.0/10). It conserves scarce IPv4 addresses but creates problems: inbound connections are blocked, geolocation is inaccurate (all CGNAT customers appear at the ISP's address), logging for law enforcement requires per-flow port logging, and gaming and VoIP can suffer due to port-restricted NAT behavior. IPv6 deployment is the long-term solution.
12216: DNSSEC (DNS Security Extensions) adds cryptographic signatures to DNS records so resolvers can verify that responses have not been tampered with in transit. Each zone signs its records with a Zone Signing Key (ZSK), and a Key Signing Key (KSK) signs the ZSK, forming a chain of trust anchored at the IANA root. A validating resolver checks these signatures and rejects records with invalid signatures. DNSSEC does not encrypt DNS traffic (that is the role of DoH/DoT) but it does guarantee data integrity and authenticity, preventing cache poisoning and man-in-the-middle attacks on DNS.
12217: Both DoH (RFC 8484) and DoT (RFC 7858) encrypt DNS queries to prevent eavesdropping and tampering by the network. DoT runs DNS over a dedicated TLS connection on port 853, making it easy for network administrators to monitor and filter. DoH encapsulates DNS queries inside HTTPS on port 443, blending with web traffic and making it much harder for enterprise DNS filtering tools to intercept. Defenders should be aware that DoH can bypass DNS-based security controls; enterprise policy should explicitly handle DoH, either via proxy interception or allowlisting approved resolvers.
12218: TLS 1.3 reduces the handshake to 1 Round-Trip Time (1-RTT) instead of TLS 1.2's 2-RTT. The client sends a ClientHello with supported cipher suites and key shares (Diffie-Hellman public values). The server responds with ServerHello plus its key share, immediately deriving a shared secret, and then sends encrypted Certificate, CertificateVerify, and Finished messages. The client verifies the server certificate, sends its own Finished, and both sides derive session keys. TLS 1.3 removed vulnerable options (RSA key exchange, CBC cipher suites, SHA-1), mandates forward secrecy via ephemeral DH, and provides 0-RTT session resumption.
12219: Certificate Transparency (CT) is an open framework (RFC 6962) that requires Certificate Authorities to log every TLS certificate they issue to publicly auditable, append-only logs. Browsers (via Chrome and Safari policies) refuse certificates not present in CT logs. Security teams can monitor CT logs (e.g., via crt.sh or Cert Spotter) to detect unauthorized certificates issued for their domains — an early warning of phishing infrastructure or CA compromise. CT does not prevent mis-issuance but makes it detectable and auditable.
12220: Mutual TLS (mTLS) requires both the client and the server to present and validate X.509 certificates during the TLS handshake, providing bidirectional authentication. It is used in service-to-service communication in zero-trust and microservices architectures (e.g., Istio service mesh), machine-to-machine API authentication, and IoT device-to-cloud communication. Unlike standard TLS where only the server is authenticated, mTLS ensures that only authorized clients can connect, eliminating credential-stuffing and unauthorized access at the transport layer.
12221: WireGuard is a modern VPN protocol implemented in approximately 4,000 lines of kernel code (versus hundreds of thousands for IPsec), making it far easier to audit. It uses a fixed, opinionated cryptographic suite: Curve25519 for key exchange, ChaCha20-Poly1305 for encryption, and BLAKE2 for hashing — no algorithm negotiation means fewer misconfigurations. WireGuard operates at Layer 3, is connectionless (no handshake state until data flows), and is significantly faster than OpenVPN and competitive with IPsec. Its simplicity and performance make it increasingly preferred for both remote-access and site-to-site VPNs.
12222: Zero-trust (ZTA) is a security model that eliminates implicit trust based on network location — being inside the corporate perimeter no longer grants access. Its core principles are: (1) verify explicitly — authenticate and authorize every request based on identity, device health, and context; (2) use least-privilege access — grant only the minimum permissions needed for each task; (3) assume breach — design as if attackers are already inside, segment networks, and monitor everything. ZTA is implemented through identity providers, device compliance checks, microsegmentation, ZTNA, and continuous monitoring.
12223: Microsegmentation applies granular firewall policies at the workload or application level, not just at network perimeters. Instead of allowing free lateral movement within a flat data center VLAN, microsegmentation enforces policy between individual VMs, containers, or processes. When an attacker compromises one workload, they cannot pivot to adjacent ones because east-west traffic is controlled and inspected. Technologies include host-based firewalls (Windows Firewall, iptables), VMware NSX distributed firewall, and Illumio. Effective microsegmentation requires accurate application flow mapping before policies can be written.
12224: A traditional stateful firewall tracks connection state (five-tuple: src/dst IP, src/dst port, protocol) and enforces rules based on IP addresses and ports. An NGFW adds Layer 7 application identification through deep packet inspection (DPI), recognizing applications regardless of port, user identity integration (who, not just what IP), SSL/TLS decryption and inspection, integrated IPS signatures, URL categorization, and malware sandboxing for unknown files. NGFWs enforce policy based on application plus user plus content, catching threats that port-based firewalls miss entirely.
12225: An IDS (Intrusion Detection System) passively monitors a copy of traffic (via a network TAP or span port) and generates alerts when suspicious patterns are detected; it takes no action on the live traffic. An IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) sits inline in the traffic path and can block, drop, or reset connections in real time. Use an IDS when you need visibility without risk of blocking legitimate traffic or causing performance impact — useful in early deployment phases. Use an IPS in inline mode once detection fidelity is tuned and false positive rates are acceptable. Most modern deployments run NGFWs with integrated IPS in inline mode.
12226: A SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) aggregates logs from firewalls, endpoints, identity systems, cloud services, and applications, normalizes them into a common format, and applies correlation rules to detect suspicious patterns. Effective detection engineering goes beyond vendor-provided signatures: it maps detections to the MITRE ATT&CK framework, writes rules for observed attacker TTPs (tactics, techniques, procedures), regularly tunes to reduce false positives, and measures detection coverage gaps. Good SIEM hygiene requires reliable log ingestion (no silent failures), retention policies for forensic needs, and baseline behavioral analytics to catch anomalies.
12227: Defense-in-depth is a security strategy that layers multiple independent controls so that the failure of any single control does not result in a breach. Layers typically include: perimeter controls (NGFW, DDoS mitigation), network segmentation (VLANs, microsegmentation), endpoint protection (EDR, host-based firewall), identity controls (MFA, PAM, least-privilege), application security (WAF, secure coding, SAST/DAST), data protection (encryption at rest and in transit, DLP), and detection and response (SIEM, SOC). The goal is to make attackers overcome multiple barriers, increasing the chance of detection before impact.
12228: Evil-twin detection relies on monitoring the RF environment for access points broadcasting your legitimate SSIDs but with different BSSIDs (MAC addresses) or on unexpected channels. A Wireless IDS/IPS (WIDS/WIPS) continuously scans all channels, compares observed BSSIDs against an authorized device inventory, and alerts on unknown APs using authorized SSIDs. Additional signals include client complaints of certificate errors (the evil-twin's RADIUS cert will be untrusted), unusual authentication failures, and wired-side detection of rogue APs plugged into switch ports via 802.1X port security. Deploying WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise with EAP-TLS and mutual certificate authentication prevents clients from associating even if they connect to an evil-twin.
12229: A deauthentication (deauth) flood sends spoofed 802.11 management frames that force clients to disconnect from a legitimate AP, causing a denial of service. Because legacy 802.11 management frames are unauthenticated and unencrypted, any attacker within radio range can spoof them. The primary mitigation is enabling 802.11w (Protected Management Frames / PMF), which cryptographically authenticates deauth and disassociation frames so spoofed ones are discarded. WPA3 mandates PMF. Additionally, a WIPS can detect and alert on deauth floods by identifying abnormal management frame rates from unexpected sources.
12230: The PMKID attack (discovered 2018) allows an attacker to capture a single EAPOL frame from the access point — without needing to observe a client handshake — and attempt offline dictionary attacks against the captured PMKID value, which is derived from the PSK. Defense involves: using a long, random WPA2-PSK passphrase (20+ characters of mixed types) to resist brute force, or better yet, migrating to WPA3-SAE, which eliminates offline dictionary attacks entirely by using a Dragonfly key exchange that requires online interaction. Enterprise networks should use WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise with 802.1X, which avoids shared keys altogether.
12231: EAP-TLS requires both the RADIUS server and the client to present valid X.509 certificates, providing mutual authentication with no shared secret or password involved — compromising a user's password has no effect. PEAP-MSCHAPv2 creates an encrypted TLS tunnel to the RADIUS server (using only the server's certificate) and then authenticates the user inside with a username and password via MSCHAPv2. EAP-TLS is significantly more secure: it eliminates password-based attacks, credential phishing, and requires a PKI but provides hardware-bound authentication. PEAP-MSCHAPv2 is simpler to deploy but has known weaknesses in MSCHAPv2 that can be exploited if the server certificate is not properly validated by the client.
12232: SAE (Dragonfly key exchange, RFC 7664) replaces the WPA2 four-way handshake for Personal networks. Unlike PSK, SAE provides forward secrecy — each connection derives a unique session key, so capturing past Wi-Fi traffic cannot be decrypted even if the password is later known. SAE is also resistant to offline dictionary attacks because it requires online interaction with the AP to test each password guess, rate-limiting brute force attempts. Additionally, SAE eliminates the PMKID vulnerability that allows capturing a handshake token for offline cracking. WPA3-Personal is required for any network where forward secrecy matters.
12233: A captive portal intercepts unauthenticated HTTP/HTTPS traffic and redirects users to a login or terms-acceptance page before granting internet access. It is commonly used on guest Wi-Fi. Security limitations include: it provides no encryption (the underlying connection may be unprotected PSK or open), users can bypass it by spoofing the MAC address of an already-authenticated device, HTTPS interception requires a trusted certificate, and it offers no protection against malicious clients attacking each other (client isolation is a separate control). Captive portals are an acceptable user-experience tool for guest access but must be combined with client isolation, bandwidth limits, and VLAN segregation to be meaningful security controls.
12234: IoT devices should be placed on a dedicated SSID mapped to an isolated VLAN with strict firewall ACLs that permit only the specific cloud endpoints or servers the devices legitimately need. Because IoT devices often cannot perform 802.1X authentication, use MAC Authentication Bypass (MAB) combined with device profiling (via Cisco ISE, Aruba ClearPass) to identify and categorize devices automatically. Apply microsegmentation to prevent IoT-to-IoT lateral movement within the VLAN, monitor for anomalous traffic volume or destinations, and implement firmware update processes to patch vulnerabilities in the device fleet.
12235: WPS (Wi-Fi Protected Setup) is a convenience feature that allows devices to join a WPA2 network using a PIN or button press instead of a passphrase. The WPS PIN exchange has a fundamental design flaw: the 8-digit PIN is validated in two halves (4+4 digits), reducing the brute-force search space from 100 million to roughly 11,000 combinations, making it trivially crackable. Even 'push-button' WPS creates a window of vulnerability when the button is active. Enterprise access points should have WPS disabled in all forms. Most modern enterprise APs disable WPS by default, but it is worth verifying after firmware updates.
12236: A BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) Wi-Fi policy defines rules for personal devices connecting to corporate networks. Technical controls should include: MDM enrollment (Microsoft Intune, Jamf, VMware Workspace ONE) to verify OS patch level, encryption, and screen lock before granting access; certificate-based 802.1X authentication so only MDM-enrolled devices receive the required client certificate; dynamic VLAN assignment placing personal devices on a restricted VLAN separate from corporate assets; NAC compliance checking for minimum OS version and antivirus; and conditional access policies that revoke certificates on non-compliant or lost devices. The BYOD VLAN should have firewall rules permitting only approved services.
12237: After a user successfully authenticates against the RADIUS server, the RADIUS Access-Accept packet includes Tunnel-Type (13=VLAN), Tunnel-Medium-Type (6=802), and Tunnel-Private-Group-ID (the VLAN ID or name) attributes. The wireless controller or AP reads these attributes and places the client's wireless session into the specified VLAN, which is carried back to the wired network over an 802.1Q trunk. This allows the RADIUS policy engine (e.g., Cisco ISE, Aruba ClearPass) to assign employees to VLAN 10, contractors to VLAN 20, and guests to VLAN 100 — all based on identity rather than which AP they associated with.
12238: A rogue DHCP server is an unauthorized server that responds to DHCP Discover broadcasts, assigning clients a malicious default gateway or DNS server — enabling man-in-the-middle or DNS hijacking attacks. On wired networks, DHCP snooping on the switch marks client-facing ports as untrusted and drops DHCP server responses arriving on them. On wireless networks, the AP or wireless controller should implement DHCP snooping over the wireless segment. Additionally, 802.1X authentication with VLAN assignment reduces the attacker's ability to reach broadcast domains they should not be on.
12239: For wireless troubleshooting in Wireshark, use a monitor-mode capable adapter and set the channel to match the AP under investigation. Apply capture filter 'wlan' and look for: (1) excessive Retry frames (high % indicates RF issues), (2) deauthentication or disassociation frames with reason codes, (3) DHCP request storms suggesting repeated disconnections, (4) 802.1X authentication failures (EAPOL exchanges), and (5) management frame timing (probes, auth, assoc) to pinpoint where the client gets stuck. The 'wlan.fc.retry == 1' display filter isolates retransmissions. Correlate with AP-side logs and client driver logs for root cause.
12240: Traceroute works by sending packets with increasing TTL values (starting at 1). Each router that decrements the TTL to zero returns an ICMP Time Exceeded message, revealing its IP and the round-trip time to that hop. Look for: (1) a hop with dramatically higher latency than the previous one — this identifies a congested or slow link segment; (2) consistent packet loss starting at a specific hop — indicates congestion or a firewall dropping ICMP; (3) asymmetric paths — run traceroute from both ends as forward and return paths can differ in BGP networks. On Windows use tracert; on Linux/macOS use traceroute (UDP by default) or mtr for continuous real-time path monitoring.
12241: Start with extended ping (send 100+ packets) to measure loss rate and RTT jitter. Use mtr (Linux) or WinMTR to see per-hop loss and latency simultaneously over time — this is superior to traceroute for spotting intermittent loss. Check interface error counters on switches and routers for input/output drops, CRC errors (physical layer), and queue drops (congestion). Run Wireshark on the affected segment and filter for TCP retransmissions (tcp.analysis.retransmission), duplicate ACKs, and zero-window conditions. Also check for duplex mismatch (one side half-duplex, other full-duplex) which causes collisions and late collisions visible in switch interface statistics.
12242: The MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) is the largest IP packet size a link can carry without fragmentation. Ethernet's standard MTU is 1500 bytes; jumbo frames use 9000 bytes. MTU mismatches typically occur in VPN tunnels (IPsec adds overhead, reducing effective MTU to ~1350-1400 bytes) or MPLS paths. Symptoms: large transfers fail or hang while small pings succeed ('ping works but web browsing is broken'). Diagnose with ping -f -l (Windows) or ping -M do -s (Linux) using progressively smaller sizes to find the maximum that works. The fix is to set the correct MTU on the interface or configure TCP MSS clamping on the firewall/router to prevent oversized TCP segments.
12243: Use 'dig' (Linux/macOS) or 'nslookup' to query specific DNS servers and measure response times: 'dig @8.8.8.8 example.com' shows query time at the bottom. Compare your configured DNS server to a public one — if the public DNS is much faster, suspect a slow or overloaded internal DNS server. Check for: DNSSEC validation delays if the resolver is validating signatures, DNS server CPU or memory exhaustion, high cache miss rates forcing recursive lookups, split-horizon DNS misconfiguration, and network latency to the DNS server itself. Run 'dig +trace example.com' to follow the full recursive resolution chain and identify which delegation is slow.
12244: CIDR notation combines an IP address with a prefix length (e.g., 192.168.10.0/24). The prefix length defines how many leading bits are the network portion; the rest are host bits. A /24 has 8 host bits, yielding 256 addresses (254 usable — subtract network address and broadcast). A /25 splits that into two /25 subnets of 126 usable hosts each. Useful formula: hosts per subnet = 2^(32-prefix) - 2. For planning: a /16 contains 65,534 hosts; a /22 yields 1,022 hosts; a /30 yields 2 hosts (common for point-to-point links). Supernetting (route summarization) combines contiguous subnets: four /24s summarize to a /22.
12245: The OSI model's seven layers map to practical protocols as follows: Layer 1 (Physical) — Ethernet cable, fiber, radio waves; Layer 2 (Data Link) — Ethernet frames, 802.11 Wi-Fi, VLANs, STP; Layer 3 (Network) — IP, ICMP, BGP, OSPF, ARP; Layer 4 (Transport) — TCP, UDP, SCTP; Layer 5 (Session) — TLS session establishment, RPC sessions; Layer 6 (Presentation) — TLS encryption/decryption, data encoding; Layer 7 (Application) — HTTP, DNS, SMTP, SNMP. In practice, the TCP/IP stack collapses the model into four layers: Link, Internet, Transport, and Application — but the OSI model remains the standard conceptual reference for troubleshooting and documentation.
12246: Phishing attacks use fraudulent emails, SMS, or websites to deceive users into providing credentials, installing malware, or authorizing fraudulent transactions. Organizational defenses include: email authentication (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) to block spoofed sender domains; email gateway filtering with URL sandboxing and attachment detonation; security awareness training with simulated phishing campaigns; hardware security keys (FIDO2/WebAuthn) for MFA, which prevents credential-based phishing even if credentials are entered on a fake site; DNS filtering to block known phishing domains; and EDR on endpoints to detect post-compromise malware execution. Incident response playbooks should include procedures for rapid credential reset and session token revocation.
12247: Spear phishing is targeted phishing that uses personalized information (name, role, colleagues, recent projects) to increase credibility. Business Email Compromise (BEC) is a sophisticated variant where attackers impersonate executives or vendors to authorize fraudulent wire transfers or data disclosures. Detection relies on: DMARC enforcement to reject unauthenticated emails; anomaly detection in email gateways flagging newly registered lookalike domains; behavior analytics detecting unusual login locations or times before wire transfer requests; out-of-band verification procedures (phone call to known number) for any financial transaction request received via email; and SIEM correlation rules for account takeovers preceding BEC activity.
12248: Modern ransomware follows a pattern: initial access via phishing or exposed RDP/VPN, credential theft and privilege escalation, lateral movement to maximize impact, exfiltration of sensitive data (for double extortion), and finally encryption of files with ransom demand. Defensive preparation includes: immutable offline backups tested for restoration; network segmentation limiting lateral movement; MFA everywhere, especially on remote access; EDR with behavioral detection of mass file encryption activity; patching internet-facing systems rapidly; disabling unnecessary services (SMBv1, RDP exposed to internet); incident response playbooks pre-approved for isolation actions; and cyber insurance with pre-engaged incident response retainer.
12249: A DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service) attack floods a target with traffic from thousands of compromised devices (botnet) to exhaust bandwidth, CPU, or connection table capacity.
